title: The Working Capital Conversation Most Australian SMEs Have Never Had With Their Suppliers
excerpt: Most owner-led businesses focus their cash flow effort on collecting faster from customers. The payables side, negotiating better terms with suppliers, is equally powerful and almost universally neglected.
content:
The working capital conversation in most owner-led businesses starts and stops on the same side of the ledger. How quickly are customers paying? What does the debtors ledger look like at June 30? Are there overdue accounts worth chasing before the financial year closes? These are the right questions, and attending to them is worthwhile. But they address only half of the working capital picture.

Every business has two sides to its cash cycle. The first is the money owed to you. The second is the money you owe to suppliers, and more precisely, how much time exists between consuming the goods and services you buy and the moment cash leaves your account to pay for them. This second side, the payables side, quietly shapes the opening cash position for the new financial year in a way that most owner-led businesses have never tried to influence.

For most SMEs, supplier payment terms arrive as a line printed on an invoice. Net 14 days. Net 30 days. EOM plus 7. These terms are treated as fixed and met on time. What most owners have never explored is whether those terms could be different, and what different terms would mean for the cash sitting in their operating account across any given month.

What Supplier Terms Are Actually Doing to Your Cash

Payment terms create a continuous and predictable relationship between the moment goods or services are consumed and the moment cash leaves the account to pay for them. The arithmetic of this relationship is straightforward, and its implications are consistently larger than owners expect until they calculate it.

A business purchasing $2.5 million in goods and services annually from suppliers, paying on net-14 terms, keeps roughly $96,000 in outstanding payables at any point in time. The same business paying on net-30 terms would carry $205,000 in outstanding payables. The difference, just over $109,000, represents cash sitting in the business's account under the longer terms rather than in the supplier's. No new revenue was required to create it. No debt was incurred. The business simply has a longer window between consuming its inputs and paying for them.

Extending terms from net-14 to net-30 across a meaningful share of a supplier base does not require exceptional negotiating skill. It requires the conversation, which most owner-led businesses have never tried to start.

Why the Conversation Rarely Gets Started

The most common reason supplier terms go unexamined is not that the maths is unattractive. It is that the conversation feels risky. Owners who have built supplier relationships over years are reluctant to raise something that might read as a sign of financial strain, or that might create friction in a relationship they value.

That risk is typically overstated. A supplier who has received reliable payments from the same business for three years is going to read a request to extend terms by 16 days as a commercial negotiation, not a distress signal. The largest buyers in Australia negotiate terms of net-60 or net-90 routinely. That is not a privilege of scale; it is the consequence of having asked. Most smaller businesses never ask, which is why they rarely get.

The start of a new financial year is the most natural moment for this conversation. Suppliers are reviewing commercial arrangements, pricing discussions are already underway, and a request to align terms from July 1 lands in context rather than as an out-of-the-blue ask. A business that initiates this conversation before June 30 is positioning itself to carry a stronger opening cash balance throughout the first quarter of FY27.

Where to Start and How to Approach It

Not every supplier needs this conversation. The useful starting point is the top five to ten suppliers by annual invoice volume, because that is where most of the spend sits and where term improvements produce the largest cash benefit.

Within that group, the most productive conversations are with suppliers the business has worked with for multiple years, where there is relationship capital earned through consistent, reliable payment. Suppliers who are reasonably large relative to the size of the business are also better candidates than those for whom the account is significant, because the former have more flexibility to accommodate a terms change without affecting their own cash position meaningfully.

The conversation itself is brief. Most businesses find that a direct, professional request produces a response without extended back-and-forth. The framing that works:

Most suppliers who are willing say yes quickly. Those who prefer to stay on current terms will say so without damaging the relationship.

For businesses that run this work formally, the combined cash release across a handful of key suppliers typically amounts to a meaningful working capital improvement in the opening months of the new financial year, without any change to the revenue side of the business.

faqs.q: What are supplier payment terms and how do they affect business cash flow?
faqs.a: Supplier payment terms define the time between receiving goods or services and the due date for payment. The longer the term, the more cash stays in your account during that window. At $2 million in annual supplier spend, moving from net-14 to net-30 keeps an additional $88,000 in your operating account on any given day, without any new revenue or borrowing. Most businesses have never calculated this number for their own supplier base, which is why the payables side of working capital remains the most consistently underused cash lever available.

faqs.q: Can Australian SMEs negotiate better payment terms with their suppliers?
faqs.a: Yes, and more often than owners expect. Most SME owners treat supplier terms as fixed, but they are a starting position in a commercial arrangement, not a permanent condition. Suppliers regularly extend terms for reliable, long-standing customers. The businesses that negotiate do so straightforwardly, typically framing the request as a new financial year review, and most find that a direct professional request produces a positive outcome without prolonged back-and-forth. The key is starting the conversation rather than assuming the answer.

faqs.q: Which suppliers should I approach first about extending payment terms in Australia?
faqs.a: Start with the top five to ten suppliers by annual spend, since this is where most of the payables volume sits and where term improvements produce the largest cash benefit. Within that group, prioritise long-established relationships where you have a history of reliable payment. Suppliers for whom your account represents a meaningful but not dominant share of their business tend to have more flexibility than smaller suppliers where any terms change affects their own cash significantly. A Working Capital Unlock maps the full supplier base and ranks which conversations are most worth having.

faqs.q: How much working capital can extending supplier payment terms actually release?
faqs.a: It depends on total annual supplier spend and the size of the term extension. At $3 million in annual supplier spend, moving from net-14 to net-30 across the full supplier base releases approximately $131,000 in working capital. The cash was already sitting in the payables cycle; it simply moves from the supplier's account to yours. Modest improvements across a handful of major suppliers compound into a material difference in the opening cash position for the new financial year, with no additional revenue required.

faqs.q: When is the right time to renegotiate supplier payment terms in Australia?
faqs.a: The transition between financial years is the most natural moment. Suppliers are reviewing commercial arrangements, pricing is being discussed, and a request to align terms from July 1 lands in context rather than as an unexpected ask. Initiating these conversations before June 30 means any agreed changes take effect from the first invoices of the new financial year, which is when the opening cash position is most sensitive to working capital improvements. The end of June is consistently more productive than approaching the same conversation mid-year.

faqs.q: What is a Supplier and Payables Optimisation and who is it suited to?
faqs.a: A Supplier and Payables Optimisation engagement builds a renegotiation playbook for the top twenty suppliers by spend, covering payment terms, early-pay discount structures where both parties benefit, and consolidation opportunities where the business is distributed across multiple providers for similar inputs. It suits businesses with a meaningful supplier base where terms have not been reviewed in several years and where working capital improvement is a priority for the year ahead. Details are available at the services page.

faqs.q: How does a fractional CFO help with supplier terms and payables management for SMEs?
faqs.a: A fractional CFO arrangement brings the working capital discipline that most owner-led businesses intend to maintain but rarely find time to build. This includes tracking payables days against agreed terms, identifying suppliers where renegotiation is overdue, and maintaining the relationship context that makes those conversations most likely to succeed. The payables side of working capital is one of the first areas reviewed in a new engagement, because the cash benefit is immediate and does not require any change to the revenue side of the business.
